questions: On Apple’s official website, there is (1) a centralized page that serves as a comprehensive index of the company’s product families, services, retail options, and support resources, and (2) a separate regional page detailing the terms and conditions for purchases made through Apple’s online retail operations in Singapore. Identify both of these pages and provide a structured summary that includes: key product and service categories listed on the index page; and, from the Singapore retail terms page, the standard return window, special conditions for volume purchases, price protection rules, refund limitations, and any shipping or export restrictions. Explain how each page serves a different purpose within Apple’s ecosystem.

On Apple’s official support resources for macOS, there is a collection of introductory and feature-focused sections designed to help both new and existing users understand their computers. Identify all the major categories aimed at (a) setting up and transitioning to a Mac, (b) integrating a Mac with other Apple devices, (c) highlighting the newest operating system features, (d) managing privacy and security—including credential recovery, and (e) explaining Apple’s built-in artificial intelligence system. For each category, provide its official name and summarize the key capabilities or tasks it emphasizes.

On Apple’s official support resources for macOS, there is an overview page designed to orient both new and existing Mac users. Based on that overview, what are the main help topics highlighted for users, and what does each topic specifically enable them to do (for example, setup guidance, switching platforms, cross-device features, operating system updates, privacy tools, credential recovery, model-specific guides, and AI capabilities)? Provide a structured summary covering each major topic and its core purpose.

On Apple’s official macOS support pages, there is an overview section designed to help users orient themselves to a new Mac and understand key system capabilities. Identify all the major introductory and feature-focused help topics presented in that overview for Mac users. For each topic, summarize the specific capabilities or actions highlighted (for example, setup tasks, cross-device features, security tools, operating system enhancements, password recovery options, or AI-related functionality). Your answer should comprehensively cover every distinct help topic in that overview and clearly describe the unique focus of each.

rounds.question: On Apple’s official website, there is (1) a centralized page that serves as a comprehensive index of the company’s product families, services, retail options, and support resources, and (2) a separate regional page detailing the terms and conditions for purchases made through Apple’s online retail operations in Singapore. Identify both of these pages and provide a structured summary that includes: key product and service categories listed on the index page; and, from the Singapore retail terms page, the standard return window, special conditions for volume purchases, price protection rules, refund limitations, and any shipping or export restrictions. Explain how each page serves a different purpose within Apple’s ecosystem.
checklist_width: Mentions major product categories from the index page (e.g., Mac, iPad, iPhone, Apple Watch, Apple Vision Pro, AirPods, TV & Home, Entertainment, Support).
Mentions service/support sections from the index page (e.g., AppleCare, Downloads and Software Updates, Product Manuals, Contact Support).
States the standard 14-day return policy for Singapore purchases (non-volume).
Explains volume purchase return conditions: 7-day window and 25% restocking fee.
Describes price protection: 14 calendar days, must contact Apple, limited to 10 units, excludes special sales.
Notes refund limitations: refunds to Singapore bank accounts only and domestic refunds only.
Describes shipping/export restrictions: ships only within Singapore; no freight forwarders; subject to export control laws.
checklist_depth: Apple Site Map page identified as the comprehensive index of products, services, retail, and support resources (logic: centralized hierarchical listing of all Apple offerings).
Singapore Consumer Store Terms and Conditions page identified as the regional online retail policy page (logic: governs purchases made through Apple’s Singapore online store).
rounds.rationale: The question uses deep reasoning by describing the functional roles of two distinct Apple web resources—one acting as a global hierarchical index and the other as a region-specific retail policy page—without naming them directly. This forces identification through logical categorization. It requires wide aggregation because the responder must extract multiple scattered details: numerous product/service categories from the index page and several distinct policy elements (returns, volume conditions, price protection, refunds, shipping/export limits) from the Singapore retail terms page.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Apple Site Map About Apple Apple Leadership Career Opportunities Investors Contact Apple Legal Choose Your Country or Region Product Feedback Apple Values Environment Supply C
rounds.answer_a:
The two pages requested are the **Apple Site Map** (`apple.com/sitemap/`) and the **Singapore Sales and Refunds Policy** (`apple.com/sg/shop/browse/open/salespolicies`) [1][2].

**Apple Site Map Summary**
The Site Map functions as a centralized navigational directory for Apple's web presence [1]. Key categories cataloged here include hardware portfolios (Mac, iPad, iPhone, Watch, AirPods), Entertainment and Services (Apple Music, Apple TV+, iCloud), Retail Store finders, Support documentation, and corporate values [1]. 

**Singapore Retail Terms Summary**
The Singapore Sales and Refunds page governs localized commercial transactions for online shoppers [2]. Key provisions include:
*   **Return Window:** Eligible items can be returned strictly within 14 calendar days of receiving the product [2].
*   **Volume Purchases:** Special provisions apply to volume orders. Purchasing more than four of the same item often subjects the buyer to customized return limitations or distinct contractual frameworks [2].
*   **Price Protection:** If Apple reduces a product's retail price within 14 calendar days of receipt, customers are entitled to request a refund or credit for the price difference [2].
*   **Refund Limitations:** Returns are exclusively accepted for items bought directly from Apple Store Singapore. Opened packaged software is non-returnable unless defective [2].
*   **Shipping and Export:** Apple strictly limits deliveries to addresses within Singapore. Products are explicitly sold to end-user consumers only (resale is prohibited) and remain subject to both Singaporean and U.S. export control laws [2].

**Purpose Differentiation**
These pages serve fundamentally distinct operational roles. Formatted for brand discovery, the **Site Map** is a global, user-centric portal designed to seamlessly connect customers with Apple’s vast commercial and support offerings [1]. In contrast, the **Sales and Refunds Policy** fulfills a localized legal function, establishing binding contractual mechanisms, mitigating compliance risks, and defining the specific consumer rights afforded to Singaporean residents during e-commerce transactions [2].

## References
[1] Apple Inc. "Site Map." Apple, https://www.apple.com/sitemap/
[2] Apple Inc. "Sales and Refunds - Terms and Conditions." Apple Store (Singapore), https://www.apple.com/sg/shop/browse/open/salespolicies
rounds.answer_b:
Apple operates two distinct web pages serving different ecosystem functions. The centralized **Apple Site Map** (apple.com/sitemap) acts as a comprehensive navigation hub, organizing product families into categories: Mac, iPad, iPhone, Watch, AirPods, TV & Home, Entertainment, and Accessories [1]. It also indexes services like Apple Music, Apple TV+, Apple Arcade, iCloud+, Apple One, Apple Pay, and Apple Card [1]. Retail options include the Apple Store App, Genius Bar bookings, Today at Apple sessions, financing, and refurbished products, while support resources cover Apple ID management, order tracking, and technical assistance [1].

The **Singapore Sales & Refunds Policy** (apple.com/sg/legal/sales-support/terms) governs regional online retail operations with specific commercial terms. Customers enjoy a 14-day return window for most products, with exceptions for software downloads, gift cards, and personalized items [2]. Volume purchases of 20+ units require direct coordination with Apple sales representatives under specialized terms [2]. Apple provides price protection within 14 days of purchase, refunding price differences [2]. Refunds are limited to items in original condition with all packaging and accessories; opened software is non-returnable [2]. Shipping is restricted to Singapore addresses only—P.O. boxes are prohibited—and export of purchased products requires compliance with Singapore export licensing regulations [2].

These pages serve complementary purposes: the Site Map facilitates global ecosystem navigation and discovery, while the Singapore terms page establishes legally binding regional commerce rules.

## References
[1] Apple. (n.d.). Apple Site Map. Retrieved from https://www.apple.com/sitemap/
[2] Apple. (n.d.). Apple Sales & Refunds Policy - Singapore. Retrieved from https://www.apple.com/sg/legal/sales-support/terms/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the Apple Site Map as the centralized index and a Singapore-specific Sales & Refunds/Terms page governing online retail purchases. So both pass the entity identification requirement.

Width/Completeness: Both fail multiple critical checklist items. Neither mentions the required 7-day return window and 25% restocking fee for volume purchases. Both omit key price protection constraints (10-unit limit and exclusions for special sales). Neither clearly states that refunds are limited to Singapore bank accounts/domestic refunds only. Shipping restrictions are partially described, but neither explicitly mentions the prohibition on freight forwarders. On the index page, Agent B provides slightly broader coverage of product/service categories, but both omit some required elements (e.g., Apple Vision Pro, AppleCare, downloads/software updates).

Since both responses contain significant omissions on required Singapore retail policy specifics (WIDE failures), neither delivers a fully accurate or checklist-complete answer. Although Agent B is marginally more comprehensive in product/service listing and structure, the core policy gaps are substantial in both. Therefore, this is a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.question: On Apple’s official support resources for macOS, there is a collection of introductory and feature-focused sections designed to help both new and existing users understand their computers. Identify all the major categories aimed at (a) setting up and transitioning to a Mac, (b) integrating a Mac with other Apple devices, (c) highlighting the newest operating system features, (d) managing privacy and security—including credential recovery, and (e) explaining Apple’s built-in artificial intelligence system. For each category, provide its official name and summarize the key capabilities or tasks it emphasizes.
checklist_width: Setup guidance including choosing wallpaper, changing common settings, and starting to use apps
Windows-to-Mac transition help including navigation guidance and file transfer from PC
Cross-device integration features such as viewing/controlling iPhone from Mac and copy-paste between devices
New macOS features including making phone calls from Mac, enhanced Spotlight actions, and automatic translations
Privacy and security tools including password management and private browsing
Ability to recover a recently deleted password or passkey
Model-specific getting started guide for new Mac owners
Description of Apple Intelligence as personal, private, and integrated into macOS
checklist_depth: ‘Get started with your Mac’ + focuses on initial setup and basic customization
‘Switched from Windows to Mac?’ + specifically addresses PC-to-Mac transition
‘Use your Mac with other Apple devices’ + centers on cross-device continuity features
‘What’s new in macOS Tahoe’ + dedicated to new OS design and feature updates
‘Control your privacy and security’ + emphasizes built-in protection tools
‘Recover a recently deleted password’ + specifically addresses credential recovery
‘Get to know your Mac’ + provides model-specific essentials for new devices
‘Apple Intelligence’ + identified as Apple’s integrated AI system within macOS
rounds.rationale: The question masks the entities by describing their functional roles (setup, transition, integration, OS updates, security, AI) rather than naming them directly (Deep reasoning). To answer fully, a respondent must identify every relevant support category and extract distinct feature details from each, requiring broad aggregation across multiple sections rather than a single source (Wide coverage).
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Get started with your Mac You can set up and start using your new Mac in no time. Choose a wallpaper for your desktop, change common settings, start using apps and more. Learn
rounds.answer_a:
**Get started with your Mac:** Aimed at setup and transitions, this section emphasizes migrating data from an old Mac or PC using Migration Assistant, creating user profiles, connecting accessories, and mastering basic interface elements like the Dock and Finder [1].

**Mac and your Apple devices:** This category details ecosystem integration through Continuity features. It outlines tasks like utilizing iPhone Mirroring, sharing files via AirDrop, copying text across devices using Universal Clipboard, and employing an iPad as a secondary display via Sidecar [1] [2].

**What’s new in macOS:** Designed to summarize the latest operating system advancements, it highlights major features introduced in the current release, macOS Sequoia. Key capabilities include advanced smart window tiling, Safari browsing enhancements, and the new standalone Passwords application [1] [3].

**Mac privacy and security:** Focused on system protection and access management, this section guides users through encrypting data with FileVault, securing logins with Touch ID and Passkeys, controlling app tracking permissions, and navigating Apple Account credential and password recovery processes [1] [4].

**Apple Intelligence on Mac:** This category explains Apple’s built-in generative artificial intelligence system. It highlights capabilities such as utilizing Writing Tools to proofread or summarize text, generating original visuals, and interacting with a highly context-aware Siri, while leveraging Private Cloud Compute to ensure user data remains secure and strictly private [1] [5].

## References
[1] Apple Inc. "macOS User Guide - Table of Contents." Apple Support, 2024. https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/welcome/mac
[2] Apple Inc. "Use Mac with other Apple devices." Apple Support, 2024. https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/use-mac-with-other-apple-devices-mchl1d734309/mac
[3] Apple Inc. "What's new in macOS Sequoia." Apple Support, 2024. https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/whats-new-in-macos-mchl11da1204/mac
[4] Apple Inc. "Mac privacy and security." Apple Support, 2024. https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/mac-privacy-and-security-mchl18c028cc/mac
[5] Apple Inc. "Apple Intelligence on Mac." Apple Support, 2024. https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/apple-intelligence-mchl39b56fcc/mac
rounds.answer_b:
Apple organizes its macOS support resources into distinct categories:

**(a) Setting Up and Transitioning:** "Mac Setup and Getting Started" guides new users through initial configuration, migrating data from Windows or older Macs, and basic navigation. It includes tutorials on using the Dock, Finder, and System Settings[1][2].

**(b) Device Integration:** "Continuity and Handoff" focuses on seamless workflows across Apple devices. Key features include Universal Clipboard, AirDrop, Sidecar (using iPad as second display), Handoff for transferring tasks between devices, and iPhone calls/messages on Mac[3][4].

**(c) Newest OS Features:** "What's New in macOS" highlights the latest operating system capabilities, typically organized by version (e.g., Sonoma, Ventura). It covers redesigned apps, enhanced widgets, updated productivity tools, and performance improvements[5][6].

**(d) Privacy and Security:** "Privacy and Security" manages data protection, FileVault encryption, Gatekeeper app verification, and credential recovery through Apple ID password reset, Recovery Key, and iforgot.apple.com. It also covers two-factor authentication and secure boot[7][8].

**(e) Built-in AI System:** "Apple Intelligence" explains the on-device AI system emphasizing privacy-preserving machine learning for features like Live Text, Visual Look Up, enhanced Siri suggestions, and system-wide writing assistance. It operates locally when possible and uses Private Cloud Compute for complex requests[9][10].

## References
[1] https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT201732
[2] https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT204216
[3] https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT204681
[4] https://support.apple.com/guide/macintosh/integrate-devices-mchlp3016/mac
[5] https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT213268
[6] https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-whats-new/welcome/mac
[7] https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT201236
[8] https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT212520
[9] https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT213248
[10] https://www.apple.com/apple-intelligence/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents generally identified the correct Apple macOS support sections as the core entity. However, neither precisely matched several official section names from the checklist (e.g., “Switched from Windows to Mac?”, “Get to know your Mac,” “Recover a recently deleted password,” and the specific “What’s new in macOS Tahoe”). Agent A was closer to official naming, while Agent B used more generalized or rephrased titles.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both agents failed to cover all required major categories. Neither explicitly included the standalone Windows-to-Mac transition page, the model-specific “Get to know your Mac,” nor the dedicated “Recover a recently deleted password” section as its own category. Agent A missed multiple checklist entities; Agent B also omitted several and added some loosely related or slightly inaccurate AI examples. Thus, both fail the WIDE aggregation requirement.

Finally, Presentation & UX: Both responses are clearly formatted with bold headers and concise summaries. Agent B has more citations, but citation density alone does not compensate for missing required categories. Agent A’s structure is slightly cleaner and more aligned with the user guide taxonomy, but not decisively better.

Because both agents missed multiple mandatory checklist items and neither achieved full coverage, this is a Low Quality Tie.

Describes guidance for users switching from Windows (navigation help and file transfer from PC)

Switch from Windows to Mac + Identified as the transition support section for PC users

This forces identification of the correct support page through reasoning about its purpose and structure. It requires wide aggregation (Wide) because the respondent must extract and summarize multiple distinct help topics—setup, switching, device integration, OS updates, security, password recovery, model guides, and AI—rather than relying on a single isolated fact.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Get started with your Mac You can set up and start using your new Mac in no time. Choose a wallpaper for your desktop, change common settings, start using apps and more. Learn
rounds.answer_a:
Based on the official Apple Mac Support overview page, the principal help topics designed to orient users encompass the following categories:

**Get Started with Mac:** Guides new users through initial machine setup, connecting accessories, and seamlessly switching platforms by migrating data from a Windows PC or an older Mac, while teaching fundamental navigation features like the Dock, Spotlight, and Finder [1].

**macOS and Software Updates:** Educates users on navigating the operating system, specifically emphasizing how to download and install software updates safely. It highlights migrating to the newest OS release (such as macOS Sequoia) to leverage new capabilities like smart window tiling [1] [2].

**Mac and Apple Devices:** Details the ecosystem's Continuity tools that allow users to integrate their Apple hardware. This explicitly enables cross-platform features like iPhone Mirroring, sharing files via AirDrop, copying text with Universal Clipboard, and utilizing an iPad as a secondary display [1].

**Privacy, Security, and Passwords:** Empowers users to secure their computers utilizing built-in protocols like Touch ID, FileVault encryption, and app permission controls. It also features robust credential management, outlining steps for Apple Account password recovery and centralized credential storage [1].

**Apple Intelligence:** Explains Apple’s built-in generative AI capabilities. It specifically enables users to quickly summarize or proofread text via Writing Tools, generate custom images, and interact with a highly context-aware Siri, all functioning under secure privacy standards [1] [3].

**Identify Your Mac / Model-Specific Guides:** Directs users to customized hardware manuals. This topic ensures owners can easily locate precise technical specifications, accessory compatibility charts, and hardware tutorials uniquely tailored to their exact machine, such as a MacBook Air, Mac mini, or Mac Studio [1].

## References
[1] Apple Inc. "Mac Support." Apple Support, 2024. https://support.apple.com/mac
[2] Apple Inc. "macOS Support." Apple Support, 2024. https://support.apple.com/macos
[3] Apple Inc. "Apple Intelligence." Apple Support, 2024. https://support.apple.com/apple-intelligence
rounds.answer_b:
Apple’s macOS support overview page structures help around eight core topics:

**Set up your Mac** guides initial configuration, Apple ID creation, and migrating data from Windows or another Mac, enabling new users to onboard quickly[1].

**Switch to Mac from Windows** provides platform-specific resources for transitioning users, translating Windows workflows to macOS with keyboard shortcuts and app equivalents[2].

**Continuity** details cross-device integration between Mac, iPhone, and iPad, enabling Handoff for task transfer, AirDrop, Universal Clipboard, and iPhone cellular calls/messages[3].

**What's New in macOS** spotlights the latest operating system version’s features, redesigned apps, and productivity enhancements for users after upgrading[4].

**Privacy and Security** centralizes management of FileVault encryption, Gatekeeper controls, and credential recovery through Apple ID password reset, Recovery Key setup, and iforgot.apple.com[5].

**Apple Intelligence** explains on-device AI capabilities including system-wide Writing Tools, Image Playground, enhanced Siri, and Visual Intelligence, using local processing and Private Cloud Compute for privacy[6].

**Mac User Guide & Resources** delivers comprehensive system documentation supplemented by model-specific articles for different Mac hardware[7].

**Get Support** offers troubleshooting diagnostics, repair scheduling, and direct contact options for resolving technical issues[8].

## References
[1] https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT201732
[2] https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT204216
[3] https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT204681
[4] https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-whats-new/welcome/mac
[5] https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT201236
[6] https://support.apple.com/en-us/HT213248
[7] https://support.apple.com/guide/mac/welcome/mac
[8] https://support.apple.com/repair
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the Apple macOS Support overview and structured their answers around the major help sections (setup, switching, cross-device use, updates, privacy, model guides, Apple Intelligence). So both pass the entity check.

Width/Completeness: Agent A misses or weakly covers several required checklist items. It does not explicitly mention "Recover a Recently Deleted Password or passkey," and it references macOS Sequoia instead of macOS Tahoe, omitting Tahoe-specific features like phone calls from Mac, enhanced Spotlight actions, and automatic translations. These are notable gaps and a minor inaccuracy. Agent B also does not explicitly mention "recently deleted password or passkey," but otherwise covers the required categories more distinctly (Switch from Windows, Continuity, What’s New, Privacy & Security, Apple Intelligence, model-specific guides). While Tahoe-specific features are summarized more generally, there are fewer inaccuracies.

User Experience & Presentation: Both are well structured with bold headers. However, Agent B mirrors the official section structure more cleanly, separates switching from setup, and provides clearer scannability with tighter summaries and higher citation density.

Conclusion: Both are solid, but Agent B is more complete and better structured, while Agent A has minor inaccuracies and misses key checklist details. Therefore, [[B_BETTER]].

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

rounds.answer_a:
Based on Apple’s official macOS support overview, the primary introductory and feature-focused help topics designed to orient users include the following categories:

**Get started with your Mac**: This topic serves as a foundational guide for new users. It emphasizes initial setup tasks, such as migrating data from a Windows PC or an older Mac, connecting external accessories, and mastering core macOS interface elements like the Dock, Spotlight, and Finder [1].

**What’s new in macOS**: Designed for user orientation regarding system enhancements, this section highlights the latest operating system updates. For macOS Sequoia, it details cutting-edge improvements like sophisticated smart window tiling, major Safari browser updates, and the highly anticipated standalone Passwords application [1] [2].

**Apple Intelligence on Mac**: This category focuses on Apple’s deeply integrated generative AI capabilities. It explains how users can leverage advanced Writing Tools to seamlessly rewrite, proofread, or summarize text. It also highlights interacting with a more contextually aware Siri and generating custom visuals, all while maintaining rigorous privacy standards through Private Cloud Compute [1].

**Mac and your Apple devices**: Focused on the Apple ecosystem, this section explains Continuity features. It highlights actions like seamlessly mirroring an iPhone to the Mac, legally sharing files utilizing AirDrop, effortlessly copying and pasting content across platforms via Universal Clipboard, or extending screen real estate by using an iPad as a secondary display [1].

**Mac privacy and security**: This topic empowers users to manage access and safeguard their data system-wide. It reviews built-in security tools like FileVault encryption and Touch ID, while also providing essential guidance on credential recovery, centralized password management, and controlling application tracking permissions to comprehensively defend user privacy [1] [2]. 

## References
[1] Apple Inc. "macOS User Guide." Apple Support, 2024. https://support.apple.com/guide/mac-help/welcome/mac
[2] Apple Inc. "Mac Support - Official Apple Support." Apple Support, 2024. https://support.apple.com/mac
rounds.answer_b:
Apple’s macOS support overview organizes help into eight primary topics:

**Set up your Mac** enables initial configuration, Apple ID setup, and data migration from Windows or another Mac using Migration Assistant, helping users get started quickly[1].

**Switch from Windows to Mac** translates Windows workflows to macOS, providing keyboard shortcut equivalents, app recommendations, and navigation guides for transitioning users[2].

**Continuity** connects Mac with iPhone and iPad, enabling Handoff for task continuation, Universal Clipboard, AirDrop, Sidecar for iPad-as-display, and iPhone calls/messages on Mac[3].

**What’s New in macOS** highlights the latest operating system version’s features, redesigned apps, and productivity enhancements for users after upgrading[4].

**Privacy and Security** manages FileVault encryption, Gatekeeper app controls, two-factor authentication, and credential recovery through Apple ID password reset, Recovery Key, and iforgot.apple.com access[5].

**Apple Intelligence** explains on-device AI capabilities including Writing Tools, Image Playground, enhanced Siri, Visual Intelligence, Private Cloud Compute, and privacy-preserving machine learning features[6].

**Mac User Guide** delivers comprehensive system documentation with model-specific articles for different Mac hardware, covering core functions and applications[7].

**Get Support** offers diagnostics tools, repair scheduling, warranty coverage details, and direct contact options for resolving hardware and software issues[8].

Agent B correctly identifies most of the distinct overview categories, including Switching from Windows, Continuity, Apple Intelligence, Privacy & Security, and Mac User Guide (model-based documentation). However, it adds “Get Support,” which is not part of the overview set, and does not explicitly mention recovering a recently deleted password or macOS Tahoe-specific features (calls from Mac, Spotlight actions, translations).

Agent B covers more of the required breadth (8 categories, including Windows switching and model-based guide), though still incomplete on Tahoe details and deleted password recovery.

User Experience & Presentation: Both are clearly structured and scannable. Agent B is more comprehensive and better aligned with the checklist categories, despite one extraneous topic. 

Conclusion: Agent B provides broader and more checklist-aligned coverage, while Agent A has both factual inaccuracy (wrong macOS version) and major omissions. Therefore, B is better, but not MUCH_BETTER due to its own omissions and minor scope drift.
